core_meaning: よう（<ruby>様<rt>よう</rt></ruby>）has two main lives: as a noun meaning 'appearance, manner, state' (how something looks or seems), and as a grammatical suffix/particle meaning 'like, as if, in the way of.' It bridges description and comparison — you use it to say something resembles something else or to describe how a situation appears to be.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼女<rt>かのじょ</rt></ruby>は<ruby>何<rt>なに</rt></ruby>か<ruby>悲<rt>かな</rt></ruby>しいことがあったような<ruby>顔<rt>かお</rt></ruby>をしてる。
sentences.translation: She has a look on her face like something sad happened to her.
sentences.notes: 〜ような modifies a noun (<ruby>顔<rt>かお</rt></ruby>) to mean 'a face that looks as if ~'; common in everyday observation.

sentences.jp: <ruby>空<rt>そら</rt></ruby>が<ruby>暗<rt>くら</rt></ruby>くなってきた。<ruby>雨<rt>あめ</rt></ruby>が<ruby>降<rt>ふ</rt></ruby>りそうなようだね。
sentences.translation: The sky's gotten dark. Looks like it might rain, huh.
sentences.notes: 〜ようだ at the end of a sentence expresses the speaker's inference based on visible evidence — here, the dark sky.

sentences.jp: <ruby>先生<rt>せんせい</rt></ruby>の<ruby>説明<rt>せつめい</rt></ruby>がよく<ruby>聞<rt>き</rt></ruby>こえるように、<ruby>前<rt>まえ</rt></ruby>の<ruby>席<rt>せき</rt></ruby>に<ruby>座<rt>すわ</rt></ruby>りました。
sentences.translation: I sat in a front seat so that I could hear the teacher's explanation clearly.
sentences.notes: 〜ように before a verb of action expresses purpose or goal — 'so that / in order that'; very common grammatical use.

sentences.jp: <ruby>彼<rt>かれ</rt></ruby>はまるで<ruby>子供<rt>こども</rt></ruby>のように<ruby>無邪気<rt>むじゃき</rt></ruby>に<ruby>笑<rt>わら</rt></ruby>った。
sentences.translation: He laughed with an innocence just like a child's.
sentences.notes: まるで〜のように is a classic comparison structure meaning 'just like / exactly as if'; まるで strengthens the simile.

sentences.jp: <ruby>以下<rt>いか</rt></ruby>に<ruby>示<rt>しめ</rt></ruby>すように、この<ruby>結果<rt>けっか</rt></ruby>は<ruby>仮説<rt>かせつ</rt></ruby>を<ruby>支持<rt>しじ</rt></ruby>するものである。
sentences.translation: As shown below, these results support the hypothesis.
sentences.notes: 〜に<ruby>示<rt>しめ</rt></ruby>すように is a set written phrase meaning 'as shown/indicated in ~'; typical of academic and formal documents.

dont_use: Don't confuse よう（<ruby>様<rt>よう</rt></ruby>）with みたい — while both express resemblance, よう is more neutral and works in formal/written contexts, whereas みたい is casual only. Also avoid using 〜ようだ when you have direct, personal knowledge of something; it implies inference from indirect evidence, so saying something you witnessed directly with 〜ようだ sounds oddly detached. Finally, don't mix up 〜ように（<ruby>目的<rt>もくてき</rt></ruby>）'so that' with 〜ように（<ruby>比較<rt>ひかく</rt></ruby>）'like' — context and the verb type (potential/negative verbs signal purpose) usually make it clear, but learners often conflate them.

confused_with.contrast: Both よう and みたい mean 'like / seems like,' but みたい is casual and spoken only — you'd never write みたい in a formal essay. よう works across all registers. Compare: <ruby>夢<rt>ゆめ</rt></ruby>みたいだね (casual, spoken) vs. <ruby>夢<rt>ゆめ</rt></ruby>のようだ (neutral/formal). For N3 and above, being able to swap between them by register is an important skill.

frequency: Extremely common across all registers. 〜ようだ／〜ような／〜ように appear constantly in daily speech, news, novels, and academic writing. This is core grammar-vocabulary that any intermediate learner will encounter daily.
anki_hint: Think of よう as a shape-shifter: as a noun it means 'appearance/state,' but in grammar it glues comparison (〜のように 'like ~') and purpose (〜ように 'so that ~') — mastering which role it's playing from context is the key.
